quests: Rejection of idol worship in Ur
Trial by fire - thrown into flames by Nimrod
Building the Kaaba with son Ismail
The ultimate test - willingness to sacrifice his son
feats: Destroyed the idols of his people to prove their powerlessness
Survived being thrown into a massive fire by King Nimrod through divine protection
Debated with Nimrod about the nature of God
Built the Kaaba with his son Ismail as the first house of worship
Willingly prepared to sacrifice his son Ismail upon divine command
Established the rituals of Hajj pilgrimage
name: Prophet Ibrahim
attributes: Patriarch of all three Abrahamic faiths
Model of complete submission to Allah
weapons: Rope (used to bind idols before destruction)
Knife (intended for the sacrifice, but ultimately not used)
Hammer (potentially used in construction of the Kaaba)
deeds: Rejected idol worship despite family opposition
Built the Sacred House (Kaaba) in Makkah
Willingly prepared to sacrifice his son
description: Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham), known as Khalilullah (Friend of Allah), is the father of monotheism and the patriarch of all three Abrahamic faiths. He is mentioned 69 times across 25 chapters in the Quran, more than any prophet except Moses. Ibrahim destroyed idols, survived being thrown into fire, built the Kaaba with his son Ismail, and was willing to sacrifice his son upon divine command. He is the model of complete submission (Islam) to Allah.

prophets.lineage: Ibrahim is the ancestor of all subsequent prophets in the Abrahamic tradition
descendants: Ishaq/Isaac (ancestor of Yaqub/Jacob, Yusuf/Joseph, Musa/Moses, Isa/Jesus)
prophets.title: Khalilullah - the only prophet given this title meaning 'Close Friend of Allah'

sources.title: Quran 6:75-79 - Ibrahim's contemplation of the stars, moon, and sun
Quran 21:51-67 - Destroying the idols and confronting his people
Quran 21:68-70 - Thrown into fire, Allah made it cool and safe
Quran 2:127-129 - Building the Kaaba with Ismail
Quran 37:99-107 - The sacrifice narrative (the great trial)
Quran 2:124 - Allah made Ibrahim an Imam for mankind
Quran 2:258 - Debate with the king (Nimrod) about God
Quran 14:35-41 - Ibrahim's prayers for Makkah and his descendants
Sahih Bukhari, Book 55 - Stories of the Prophets
Hadith on Ibrahim being the best of creation after Muhammad
Hadith on the rituals of Hajj established by Ibrahim
Tafsir Ibn Kathir - Stories of the Prophets

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Revelation
extendedContent.content: Ibrahim's early life is characterized by his rejection of idolatry in a society steeped in polytheism. Born in Babylon, he questioned the worship of idols from a young age, recognizing the existence of a single, supreme God. According to Islamic tradition, Ibrahim engaged in intellectual debates with his community and even his father, who was an idol maker, challenging their beliefs and advocating for monotheism. His unwavering conviction led to conflict and persecution, culminating in his being thrown into a fire by King Nimrod. Miraculously, Ibrahim survived unharmed, a testament to divine intervention and a pivotal moment solidifying his role as a prophet. This early defiance of idolatry laid the foundation for his later mission to establish monotheistic worship and guide humanity towards the one true God. His story serves as an inspiration for those who stand against injustice and uphold their beliefs in the face of adversity.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Trials
extendedContent.content: Ibrahim's life was marked by numerous trials and quests that tested his faith and obedience to God. One of the most significant was his migration from his homeland to Canaan, a journey undertaken in response to divine command. This migration symbolized his commitment to leaving behind his past and embracing a new life dedicated to God. Another profound trial was the command to sacrifice his son Ismail, a test of his ultimate devotion. Despite the immense emotional burden, Ibrahim prepared to fulfill the divine command, demonstrating his unwavering faith. However, God intervened at the last moment, replacing Ismail with a ram, signifying divine mercy and Ibrahim's successful completion of the test. Furthermore, Ibrahim, along with his son Ismail, embarked on the quest to build the Kaaba in Mecca, establishing a sacred site for monotheistic worship and a focal point for future generations of believers. These quests and trials solidified Ibrahim's status as a prophet and a model of faith and obedience.

extendedContent.title: Enduring Legacy
extendedContent.content: Ibrahim's legacy transcends religious boundaries, impacting billions of people across the globe. He is revered as the father of monotheism, the patriarch of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. His unwavering faith, obedience to God, and commitment to justice serve as an inspiration for believers worldwide. The Abrahamic covenant, established between Ibrahim and God, promises blessings to his descendants and underscores the importance of faith and righteousness. The annual Hajj pilgrimage to Mecca, centered around the Kaaba built by Ibrahim and Ismail, draws millions of Muslims each year, connecting them to the legacy of Ibrahim. Furthermore, Ibrahim's emphasis on hospitality, compassion, and charity continues to influence ethical and moral values in contemporary society. His story promotes interfaith dialogue and understanding, fostering respect and cooperation among different religious communities. Ibrahim's enduring legacy serves as a reminder of the power of faith, the importance of ethical conduct, and the potential for unity among diverse peoples.

angels.jibreel: Angel Jibreel brought divine messages to Ibrahim
angels.visitors: Angels in human form visited Ibrahim to give news of a son (Ishaq) and the destruction of the people of Lot

achievements: Destruction of idols in his community, challenging polytheism.
Survival of being thrown into a fire by King Nimrod, a testament to divine protection.
Migration from his homeland to Canaan, a journey of faith and obedience to God.
Construction of the Kaaba in Mecca with his son Ismail, establishing a sacred site for monotheistic worship.
Willingness to sacrifice his son Ismail as an act of obedience to God, demonstrating ultimate faith.
Establishment of the Abrahamic covenant with God, promising blessings to his descendants.
Hospitality towards strangers, exemplified by his welcoming of the angels in disguise.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Represents the trials and tribulations Ibrahim faced, as well as divine protection and intervention.

associations.name: Kaaba
associations.significance: Symbolizes the house of God and the center of monotheistic worship, built by Ibrahim and his son.

associations.name: Ram
associations.significance: Represents the divine substitution for Ismail during the sacrifice, a symbol of God's mercy.

associations.name: Hospitality
associations.significance: Reflects Ibrahim's generosity and kindness towards strangers, a virtue emphasized in Abrahamic traditions.

associations.name: Monotheism
associations.significance: Ibrahim is the father of monotheism, representing the belief in one God and the rejection of polytheism.

associations.name: Mecca
associations.significance: The location of the Kaaba, established as a sacred site by Ibrahim and Ismail.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Shares the same foundational narrative, including the covenant with God, willingness to sacrifice Isaac, and migration to Canaan.
Recognized as a patriarch and a model of faith, particularly for his willingness to sacrifice Isaac and his belief in God's promise.
Both figures represent a new beginning after a period of corruption, with Noah surviving the flood and Ibrahim establishing monotheism.

worshipPractices.name: Eid al-Adha
worshipPractices.description: The Feast of Sacrifice, commemorating Ibrahim's willingness to sacrifice his son. Muslims sacrifice an animal and share the meat with family, friends, and the needy.

worshipPractices.name: Hajj Pilgrimage
worshipPractices.description: The annual pilgrimage to Mecca, including rituals performed at the Kaaba, retracing the steps of Ibrahim and his family, such as the Sa'i (walking between Safa and Marwa).

worshipPractices.name: Daily Prayers (Salat)
worshipPractices.description: Muslims face the Kaaba in Mecca during their five daily prayers, acknowledging the direction established by Ibrahim as the center of monotheistic worship.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ibrahim's legacy endures as a symbol of unwavering faith, obedience to God, and the father of monotheism. He remains a unifying figure across Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, promoting interfaith dialogue and understanding. His story inspires acts of charity, sacrifice, and devotion to God in contemporary society.

shortDescription: Ibrahim, known as Abraham, is the father of monotheism in Islam, revered for his unwavering faith, obedience to God, and building the Kaaba.
subtitle: The Father of Monotheism and Friend of Allah
